question_state: The question asks for the highest daily Brent crude oil spot price, in the exact FRED/EIA series DCOILBRENTEU, during 2026-05-13 through 2026-05-15. The run established that the official series is an EIA-sourced daily Brent-Europe spot measure published through FRED, but direct target-window observations were not yet visible; the latest official data only reached 2026-05-01. To bridge that gap, the forecaster combined the official series history and EIA’s May 2026 STEO with near-real-time Brent and crude market reporting. What emerged was a picture of extreme late-April spikes fading toward an EIA-expected May average near 106, followed by a renewed rebound to roughly 107 immediately before the target window as ceasefire hopes weakened. That mix supported forecasting a weekly high slightly above 107, but not another late-April panic extreme.
factors_tracked: Official primary-source series status and latest observed DCOILBRENTEU/EIA values
Authoritative EIA forward guidance for May 2026 Brent levels
Immediate pre-window Brent price trajectory from live market proxies
Geopolitical supply-disruption risk from Strait of Hormuz / U.S.-Iran conflict
Spot-versus-futures/basis considerations for mapping Brent market quotes to the FRED/EIA spot series
Cross-check from WTI level and likely Brent premium

evidence_surfaced.source: FRED series page / FRED data page for DCOILBRENTEU, sourced from EIA
evidence_surfaced.claim: FRED/EIA’s Brent-Europe daily spot series (DCOILBRENTEU) showed the latest official observation at 118.26 USD/bbl on 2026-05-01 after late-April values above 124, proving the underlying series had recently experienced a sharp geopolitical spike.
EIA’s May 2026 Short-Term Energy Outlook projected Brent spot prices to average 106 USD/bbl in both May and June 2026, implying mid-May should center well below the late-April panic highs.

evidence_surfaced.source: TradingEconomics Brent news/price page and related market summaries cited in the run
evidence_surfaced.claim: Near-window market proxies indicated Brent fell to roughly 101 USD/bbl by 2026-05-08 and then rebounded to about 107.16 by 2026-05-12 as ceasefire hopes faded and Hormuz disruption fears returned.

A 2026-05-11 Barchart report showed June WTI at 101.26 with explicitly bullish supply-shock fundamentals tied to Hormuz closure risk, supporting a several-dollar Brent premium and a likely weekly Brent high above the May-June average.

evidence_surfaced.source: OilPrice headline/search snippets referenced late in the run
evidence_surfaced.claim: Search results also surfaced de-escalation headlines suggesting oil could fall back below 100 if the Iran conflict eased, capping confidence in any forecast far above 110.

judgment_trajectory: Initial view: start from FRED/EIA because the target is a specific official daily series; likely need to forecast because target-window observations are not directly published yet.

After retrieving the primary-source metadata and latest values: official series had spiked dramatically into late April and still sat at 118.26 on 2026-05-01, so the weekly high could not be treated as a normal low-70s/80s environment.

After reading the May STEO: EIA’s own baseline of 106 for May-June shifted the center of mass down from late-April extremes and argued against forecasting a repeat of 118-138 unless a fresh shock occurred.

After adding 2026-05-08 to 2026-05-12 market proxies: Brent appeared to have sold off toward ~101 and then rebounded to ~107 just before the target window, making a local high a bit above 107 more likely than either sub-100 or a return to 120+.

Final synthesis: with bullish geopolitical momentum but no direct evidence of a new breakout above 110 before the window, the best point estimate became a modest overshoot of the STEO average and pre-window proxy level: 108.74 USD/bbl.
what_worked: Using the primary source first established the exact series definition, latest official observation date, and the fact that the answer had to be forecast rather than directly looked up.
EIA’s May 2026 STEO provided the strongest same-source numerical anchor for expected mid-May Brent levels.
Near-window market proxy lookups from TradingEconomics and news summaries were effective for estimating direction and magnitude immediately before 2026-05-13.
Cross-checking with Barchart/WTI and EIA commentary on extreme spot tightness helped translate general crude strength into a plausible Brent spot high.
what_misled: Repeated attempts to pull the FRED CSV/direct data feed failed or timed out, consuming budget without improving the core estimate.
Browser snapshots of the FRED data page mostly repeated metadata and maintenance notices rather than yielding incremental dated observations.
Bloomberg was blocked by a 403/CAPTCHA page, so it did not provide usable confirmation.
Some search results returned stale or malformed FRED snippets (including odd old observation snippets), which were not useful for the target-window forecast.
unresolved_concerns: How tightly the FRED/EIA Brent-Europe spot series tracks the quoted Brent futures/CFD/news prices during this dislocated period, especially given unusual spot-futures spreads in April 2026.
Whether events during 2026-05-13 to 2026-05-15 trigger either a fresh geopolitical spike above 110 or a sudden de-escalation back toward 100.
Whether additional official EIA daily postings for early/mid-May would have shown the rebound already embedded in the physical spot series rather than only in market proxies.
